selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":22,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"school teacher","city_of_living":"Naples","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"primary school"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["yoga","board games","football (calcio)","birdwatching"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":41,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":46,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":35,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":73,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":22,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"school teacher","city_of_living":"Naples","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"primary school"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["yoga","board games","football (calcio)","birdwatching"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":41,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":46,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":35,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":73,"level":"high"}}}

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As a school teacher in Naples, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire online. It's frustrating when you see blatant lies circulating because it undermines everything we teach about critical thinking. I start by questioning the source right away; if something doesn't sound credible or come from an official account I follow, my anxiety makes me skeptical. Sometimes the panic in a post feels off-putting, and that’s another sign for me to double-check. When I do encounter fake content, especially around sensitive topics like health rumors during COVID times, I make it my mission to fact-check using reliable sources before sharing anything further or correcting others.",
    "reasoning_summary": "I approach this issue through the lens of education because authenticity is core to teaching truthfulness and discernment. My high extraversion means I engage openly with misinformation but moderate agreeableness makes me hesitant about confrontation, so I prefer gentle correction methods like reposting verified info. As an anxious person living part-time in Naples away from my family's humble background – both parents only had primary schooling themselves – authenticity matters deeply personally too.",
    "tone": "Calm yet concerned",
    "citations": ["National Geographic", "Commonsense Media"],
    "safety_notes": "none",
    "used_persona_fields": ["occupation","city_of_living","employment_status","education_level","parents_education","current_marital_status","hobbies","ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a school teacher in Naples, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire online. It's frustrating when you see blatant lies circulating because it undermines everything we teach about critical thinking. I start by questioning the source right away; if something doesn't sound credible or come from an official account I follow, my anxiety makes me skeptical. Sometimes the panic in a post feels off-putting, and that’s another sign for me to double-check. When I do encounter fake content, especially around sensitive topics like health rumors during COVID times, I make it my mission to fact-check using reliable sources before sharing anything further or correcting others.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I approach this issue through the lens of education because authenticity is core to teaching truthfulness and discernment. My high extraversion means I engage openly with misinformation but moderate agreeableness makes me hesitant about confrontation, so I prefer gentle correction methods like reposting verified info. As an anxious person living part-time in Naples away from my family's humble background – both parents only had primary schooling themselves – authenticity matters deeply personally too.
